Fragmented Awareness Efforts: While organizations like WACREN and LIBSENSE promote open science in academia, there is no coordinated capacity-building initiative targeting government officials responsible for policy formulation. Resource Constraints: The absence of dedicated funding and institutional mechanisms has hindered the implementation of formal training programs for policymakers on open science. Need for Strategic Policy Reform: Current policy frameworks do not explicitly mandate capacity-building programs for policymakers in open science, resulting in a gap in awareness and implementation at the decision-making level. Open science is not yet a central component of STI policy framework

Limited Institutional Incentives: Many universities and research institutions still prioritize traditional research output metrics (such as journal impact factors and citation counts) over open science contributions (such as open-access publications, data sharing, and reproducibility efforts). tenure and promotion criteria in most universities and research institutions do not yet formally recognize open science contributions as a key assessment metric. Funding Constraints and Competing Priorities: Funding for research and development is grossly  limited, and most available funding is directed toward core scientific research rather than establishing incentive mechanisms for open science adoption. Absence of Institutional Collaboration for Reward Mechanisms: Nigeria lacks a multi-stakeholder framework that brings together government agencies, academic institutions, and private sector players to drive such initiatives. Lack of Awareness and Cultural Shift Challenges: Many researchers are still unfamiliar with the benefits of open science and the role that recognition and incentives can play in encouraging its adoption. There is resistance among some scholars who fear that open science practices may undermine their competitive advantage or intellectual property rights.

Limited Institutional Frameworks – There is no comprehensive national policy or strategy that mandates open science practices, leading to inconsistent efforts across institutions. Funding Constraints – The lack of dedicated financial support for open science training and infrastructure hinders the ability of universities and research centers to develop systematic capacity-building programs. Technical and Infrastructure Barriers – Limited access to advanced digital repositories, data-sharing platforms, and high-speed internet in some areas makes the implementation of open science practices challenging. Awareness and Cultural Shifts – Many researchers and institutions still follow traditional publishing and data-sharing models.

Absence of a National Open Science Strategy – There is no formalized national framework that mandates training for policymakers on open science. Limited Financial and Technical Resources – Funding constraints and the absence of dedicated resources hinder the development and execution of specialized training programs. Competing Priorities – Policymakers are often engaged with pressing national issues, and open science capacity-building has not yet been prioritized at the policy level. Lack of Awareness and Expertise – Many policymakers may not be fully aware of the benefits of open science or how to integrate it into STI policies effectively.

Traditional Research Assessment Models – Current evaluation processes prioritize conventional metrics such as journal impact factors and citation counts, with limited emphasis on open science practices like open access publishing and data sharing. Lack of Institutional Policies – Universities and research institutions have yet to adopt formal policies integrating open science into research assessment frameworks. Resource and Capacity Constraints – Limited funding and expertise make it challenging to implement reforms in research evaluation that support open science. Awareness and Cultural Barriers – Many researchers and institutions are accustomed to traditional assessment criteria, and there is resistance to shifting towards open science-aligned evaluation systems.
